title: Africa
subtitle: Bronze, stone, cloth, and mud — craft from across the continent.
intro: Africa's art tradition is vast and old. These pieces range from 9th-century Igbo bronzes to the largest mud-brick building in the world.

works.title: Bronze Head from Ife
works.desc: Naturalistic portraits cast in brass with lost-wax technique. When first seen in Europe they were disbelieved as African work.

works.title: Benin Bronzes
works.desc: Plaques that once covered pillars of the royal palace in Benin City. Looted in 1897; still being repatriated today.

works.title: Bronze Head of Queen Idia
works.desc: Portrait of the first Queen Mother of Benin, cast to sit on an ancestral altar. One of the most famous of the Benin bronzes.

works.title: Igbo-Ukwu Bronze
works.desc: Lost-wax castings of startling delicacy — three centuries before Ife, and predating most European bronzework of the same technical sophistication.

works.title: Great Zimbabwe
works.desc: Dry-stone walls built without mortar, enclosing a trade capital that controlled the gold road to the Indian Ocean.

works.title: Great Mosque of Kilwa
works.desc: Coral-stone mosque on an island that dominated Indian Ocean trade. Ibn Battuta called Kilwa "one of the most beautiful cities in the world."

works.title: Rock-Hewn Churches of Lalibela
works.desc: Eleven churches carved downward into solid rock. Meant as a new Jerusalem after pilgrimage routes were cut off.

works.title: Great Mosque of Djenné
works.desc: The world's largest mud-brick building. Every spring the whole town replasters it by hand in a single day.

works.title: Dogon Cliff Villages
works.desc: Mud granaries and shrines stacked along the Bandiagara Escarpment. The Dogon moved here fleeing forced conversion and stayed half a millennium.

works.title: Kente Cloth
works.desc: Hand-woven silk and cotton strips sewn together into royal cloth. Each pattern has a name and a proverb behind it.

works.title: Obelisks of Aksum
works.desc: Granite stelae marking royal graves; the tallest once stood nearly a hundred feet high. One was looted by Mussolini, returned in 2005.

works.title: Nkisi Power Figure
works.desc: Wooden figures bristling with nails and blades. Each nail driven in activated a pledge, a curse, or a contract witnessed by the spirit inside.

works.title: Senufo Sculpture
works.desc: Elongated female figures and carved divination stools used by the Poro and Sandogo societies in Côte d'Ivoire and Mali.

works.title: Fang Reliquary (Byeri)
works.desc: Carved guardian heads placed atop boxes of ancestral bones. Picasso saw one in 1907 and the Demoiselles d'Avignon followed.

works.title: Akan Goldweights
works.desc: Brass figurines used to weigh gold dust. Each one is also a proverb — the weight you balance against is a riddle.

works.title: Lydenburg Heads
works.meta: Early Iron Age · 500 CE · South Africa
works.desc: Terracotta heads found buried under a farm in Mpumalanga — the earliest known African figurative sculpture south of the equator.

works.title: San Rock Art
works.meta: San · up to 30,000 years old · Southern Africa
works.desc: Eland, hunters, and trance figures painted on rock shelters from Namibia to the Drakensberg. One of humanity's longest unbroken painting traditions.

works.title: Golden Stool of the Asante
works.desc: Sacred seat that is said to contain the soul of the Asante nation. No one — not even the king — sits on it. A British demand to do so in 1900 sparked war.

works.title: Ibeji Twin Figures
works.desc: When a twin dies, a carved figure stands in — fed, dressed, and cared for like the living. The Yoruba have one of the highest twinning rates in the world.

works.title: Larabanga Mosque
works.desc: A mud-and-stick mosque painted white, bristling with wooden supports. The oldest mosque in West Africa still in its original form.

works.title: Queen Mother Pendant Mask (Iyoba)
works.desc: Ivory mask worn on the belt of the Oba of Benin. The forehead tiaras are miniature Portuguese heads — trading partners carved as ornament.

works.title: Nok Terracotta
works.desc: Hollow terracotta heads with triangular eyes and pierced pupils. The oldest known figurative sculpture in sub-Saharan Africa.

works.title: Mapungubwe Hill
works.desc: A sandstone hill-top capital that controlled Indian Ocean gold and ivory trade centuries before Great Zimbabwe. A golden rhinoceros was dug up from a royal grave here.

works.title: Ndop — Kuba King Portrait
works.meta: Kuba Kingdom · 17th c. onward · DR Congo
works.desc: Wooden royal portraits showing each Kuba king with a personal emblem — a drum, an anvil, a game board. Stand-ins who keep the king present after death.

works.title: Baule Sculpture
works.desc: Delicate wooden figures of "spirit spouses" from a parallel world, and calm portrait masks carved for Goli festivals. Among the most influential African sculptural traditions.

works.title: Asante Jewelry
works.desc: Gold jewelry for the chief and his court — beads, pectorals, rings cast with the same lost-wax technique as the goldweights. Sumptuary law regulated who could wear what.

works.title: Ethiopian Processional Cross
works.desc: Hand-held metal crosses carried at the front of church processions. Each is unique — geometries nested inside geometries, hammered out by a parish smith.

works.title: Brandberg Rock Paintings
works.meta: San · up to 2,000 years old · Namibia
works.desc: The Brandberg Massif hides thousands of rock paintings, most famously the "White Lady" — not a lady at all, but a medicine figure. Painted for ritual, not ornament.

works.title: Twyfelfontein Petroglyphs
works.desc: Thousands of rock engravings of giraffes, rhinos, and lions on sandstone. A seal, 900 km from the nearest sea, suggests long-distance travel — or long memory.

works.title: Red Bird
works.desc: Chris Ofili's paintings layer resin-coated elephant dung onto glittering collage. His work interrogates Black identity and the politics of representation in Britain.

works.title: Shona Stone Sculpture
works.desc: A post-colonial sculptural tradition carved from serpentine and springstone. Figures of spirits and ancestors, often semi-abstracted — now Zimbabwe's most recognized art export.

works.title: Adinkra Symbols
works.desc: A system of visual proverbs stamped or woven into cloth — each glyph carrying a name and an aphorism. Worn at funerals; later embraced globally as a Pan-African vocabulary.

works.title: Great Mosque of Kano
works.desc: A mud-and-stick Friday mosque rebuilt many times across six centuries. The current building rose after a 1937 fire; minarets slender as reeds above a walled city.

works.title: Kuba Appliqué Textiles
works.meta: Kuba Kingdom · 17th c. onward · DR Congo
works.desc: Raffia cloth embroidered with cut-pile geometrics. Men weave the base; women embroider. No two patterns repeat — each is a one-time composition.

works.title: Tuareg Metalwork
works.desc: Silver crosses, amulet cases, saddle fittings. Each Tuareg town has a signature cross shape; the Agadez cross is the most widely reproduced.

works.title: Akuaba
works.desc: Wooden fertility figures carried by women hoping to conceive. Small, abstracted, round-faced. Carved also now for collectors; the sacred ones are never sold.

works.title: Djinguereber Mosque
works.desc: Commissioned by Mansa Musa after his pilgrimage to Mecca. An Andalusian architect built it in earth and fossil limestone — one of three medieval mosques still standing in Timbuktu.

works.title: Sowei Helmet Mask
works.desc: A black helmet mask worn in the Sande women's society initiation — the only African mask tradition danced exclusively by women, representing ideals of beauty and comportment.

works.title: Zulu Beadwork
works.desc: Glass trade beads sewn onto leather and cotton in geometric patterns — colors and combinations encoding courtship messages ("love letters" in bead).

works.title: Fasil Ghebbi
works.desc: A fortress-city of stone castles built by a succession of Solomonic emperors. The European-looking keeps are misleading — they're Ethiopian invention, not import.

works.title: Cairo Citadel
works.desc: Saladin's fortress above old Cairo, topped centuries later by the Mosque of Muhammad Ali with its tall twin minarets — a silhouette that defines the skyline.

works.title: Osun-Osogbo Sacred Grove
works.desc: A forest along the Oshun River filled with sculptures revived by the Austrian-Yoruba artist Susanne Wenger and local carvers — the last surviving intact sacred grove of the Yoruba.

works.title: Cape Coast Castle
works.desc: A whitewashed castle on the Gulf of Guinea used for the Atlantic slave trade — enslaved Africans passed through its "Door of No Return." Now a museum, with whitewash still yearly.

works.title: Swahili Stone Towns
works.desc: Coral-stone trading towns — Lamu, Pate, Kilwa — with carved wooden doors studded in brass, Indian Ocean dhows in their harbors, and mosques whose minarets face inland.
